---
title: "pU-QTL mapping — beta-binomial model"
author: "XSun"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  workflowr::wflow_html:
    code_folding: hide
    toc: true
---

```{r setup, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE,
                      fig.align = "center", fig.width = 8, fig.height = 5)
```

# Overview

The [binomial GLM](qtl_mapping_binomial_model.html) assumes no overdispersion —
its permutation calibration check failed (permuted-null genomic inflation
$\lambda \approx 3.4$, should be $\approx 1$). This page describes the
**beta-binomial** extension, which models the extra cell-line-to-cell-line
variance directly rather than assuming pure binomial sampling noise.

Sites tested: the same **5-fold union (≥ 1 fold)** set,
**`r format(12416, big.mark=",")` sites**. Samples: the same **50 genotyped
cell lines**. Covariates: the same **M-value PCs** (below).

# The model

For a given pU site, let individual $i$ have $y_i$ converted reads out of
$n_i$ total reads, but now allow the modification rate itself to vary around
its mean $\mu_i$ (a Beta-distributed mixing probability):

$$
p_i \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha_i, \beta_i), \qquad y_i \mid p_i \sim \text{Binomial}(n_i, p_i)
$$
$$
\text{logit}(\mu_i) = \beta_0 + \beta_g G_i + z_i^\top\gamma, \qquad
\mu_i = \frac{\alpha_i}{\alpha_i+\beta_i}, \qquad
\phi = \frac{1}{\alpha_i+\beta_i+1}
$$

Marginalizing over $p_i$ gives the **beta-binomial** distribution for $y_i$,
with the same mean model as the binomial but a different variance:

$$
\text{Var}(y_i) = n_i\mu_i(1-\mu_i)\big[\,1+(n_i-1)\phi\,\big]
$$

- $\phi \in [0,1)$ — the **overdispersion / intra-class-correlation**
  parameter: how much the $n_i$ reads at one cell line behave like correlated
  (not independent) trials. $\phi=0$ recovers the binomial exactly.
- The bracketed term $1+(n_i-1)\phi$ is a **variance inflation factor** — the
  same form as the design effect in clustered/survey data. It grows with
  depth $n_i$, so high-depth cell lines are *not* automatically down-weighted
  as much as the binomial would assume, once $\phi>0$.
- One $\phi$ per site (not per cell line, not per SNP) — a single dispersion
  shared across the site's 50 observations.

# Is the data overdispersed?

For each site, fit the covariates-only **null** model (no genotype) under
both the binomial and the beta-binomial, via maximum likelihood — a single
joint fit of $(\beta_0,\gamma,\phi)$ gives both $\hat\phi$ and its
log-likelihood $\text{logLik}_{BB}$ together; a separate binomial fit
($\phi=0$) gives $\text{logLik}_{B}$. Then test $H_0:\phi=0$ with a
**boundary-corrected LRT** — because $\phi\ge0$ is a boundary parameter, the
usual $\chi^2_1$ reference is replaced by a 50:50 mixture of a point mass at
0 and $\chi^2_1$ (Self & Liang 1987):

$$
\Lambda = 2(\text{logLik}_{BB} - \text{logLik}_B), \qquad
p = \tfrac12\, P(\chi^2_1 > \Lambda)
$$

```r
nf  <- fit_null_betabin(y, n, X)                 # joint MLE: beta0, gamma, phi
stat <- max(2 * (nf$logLik_bb - binom_loglik(y, n, X)), 0)
p <- 0.5 * pchisq(stat, df = 1, lower.tail = FALSE)
```

Across the union sites: the **median site has $\hat\phi \approx 0$**
(binomial is adequate for most sites), but the distribution has a heavy right
tail — **~22% of sites are significantly overdispersed** (FDR<0.05), with
$\hat\phi$ up to ~1. See the [results
page](qtl_mapping_summary_betabinomial.html) for the full distribution.

# QTL mapping: the beta-binomial score test

Testing $\beta_g$ per (site, cis-SNP) pair the same way as the binomial (an
LRT refit per SNP) would require re-fitting the beta-binomial's joint MLE for
every cis-SNP — far too slow to permute. Instead we use a **Rao score test**,
exactly analogous to the binomial's, with every term divided by the
overdispersion factor $c_i = 1+(n_i-1)\phi$:

$$
r_i = \frac{y_i - n_i\hat\mu_i}{c_i}, \qquad
w_i = \frac{n_i\hat\mu_i(1-\hat\mu_i)}{c_i}, \qquad
U = G^\top r, \qquad V = G^\top\!\text{diag}(w)\,G - (\cdots),
\qquad U^2/V \sim \chi^2_1
$$

(The binomial score test is the special case $\phi=0 \Rightarrow c_i=1$.) This
was validated against `aod::betabin`'s full-vs-null LRT on simulated data —
matching rank order and $\log_{10}(p)$ within ~0.03 outside the extreme tail.

```r
nf  <- fit_null_betabin(y, n, X)      # phi estimated once (the slow joint MLE)
p_snp <- score_pvals(nf, G)           # per cis-SNP, same engine as the binomial
```

# Significance

Same three-step procedure as the binomial model, with one addition to keep
the permutation feasible.

## 1. Making the permutation affordable: hold $\phi$ fixed

A full beta-binomial MLE refit is ~20-30x slower than the binomial's
`glm.fit` (a joint numerical optimization over $\beta_0,\gamma,\phi$ vs. a
single IRLS). Refitting it for every one of 1000 phenotype permutations per
site would be far too slow. Since the score test treats $\phi$ as a fixed
nuisance parameter, we **estimate $\phi$ once per site**, then hold it fixed
across permutations — each permutation then only needs to refit the
**mean**, which is a plain **weighted binomial GLM** on $y/n$ with prior
weights $n_i/c_i$ (this solves the beta-binomial mean equation exactly for
fixed $\phi$):

```r
nf_full <- fit_null_betabin(y, n, X)          # slow: estimate phi (once)
phi <- nf_full$phi
# fast: refit only the mean, phi fixed -- ~30x faster, reused every permutation
nf_perm <- bb_score_setup(y_perm, n_perm, X, phi)
```

This fixed-$\phi$ mean fit was validated against the full joint MLE (same
score p-values, max $|\log_{10}(\text{ratio})|=0.013$) and is ~34x faster
(~0.6ms vs. ~20ms per fit) — making the permutation beta-approximation below
computationally feasible.

## 2. Permutation as a calibration check

Same audit as the binomial: permute the phenotype across the 50 cell lines
(5 seeds), refit **fresh** (phi re-estimated under each permutation — the
correct test, not the fixed-phi speed trick above, which is only used
*within* a site's own permutation pass in step 3), collect nominal p-values,
check $\lambda\approx1$.

**Result** (5kb, 3 PCs — see the [results
page](qtl_mapping_summary_betabinomial.html#calibration) for detail): permuted-null
$\lambda$ drops from the binomial's **~3.4 to ~2.0** — a substantial
improvement, but not full calibration. Candidate reasons the residual
inflation remains: the score test is still asymptotic ($\chi^2$) and may not
be exact at $n=50$; $\phi$ is plugged in as if known, ignoring its own
estimation uncertainty; and $1+(n-1)\phi$ is one specific overdispersion
structure that may not fit every site exactly.

## 3. Within-site correction (permutation beta-approximation)

Identical procedure to the binomial: minimum nominal p across a site's
cis-SNPs, permutation null of that minimum (**using the fast fixed-$\phi$
refit**, 1000x), Beta(a,b) fit, adjusted per-site p from the Beta CDF.

## 4. Across-site correction

BH-FDR (and q-values) over the per-site adjusted p, same as the binomial.

# A practical limitation: high PC counts

The beta-binomial's joint MLE estimates $k+2$ parameters ($k$ PCs + intercept
+ $\phi$) on only 50 samples. Unlike the binomial's IRLS (which almost always
returns *some* fit, even an overfit one), the beta-binomial's numerical
optimizer frequently fails to converge once $k\gtrsim10$ — the number of
sites it can fit collapses from ~11,000 (k=3) to ~40 (k=15). So the
beta-binomial is only usable at **low PC counts (3-7)**, which is
conveniently also where QTL discoveries peak for both models. See the
[results page](qtl_mapping_summary_betabinomial.html) for the sites-fit table
and a worked example of a site that fails to converge.

# Data

- **Sites**: 5-fold union, ≥ 1 fold — `r format(12416, big.mark=",")` pU sites.
- **Phenotype**: per site, per cell line — converted reads $C$ ($=y$) out of
  total depth $N$ ($=C+T$), identical to the binomial model (no
  transformation).
- **Genotypes**: cis SNPs within a window of each site (±5/20/50/100kb),
  common variants (MAF≥0.05) from the 50 cell lines' genotypes.
- **Covariates $z$**: M-value phenotype PCs — identical construction to the
  [binomial model](qtl_mapping_binomial_model.html#Covariates:_M-value_phenotype_PCs)
  (site-specific $(\pi_s, 1-\pi_s)$ pseudocount), swept over
  $k \in \{3,5,7,10,15,20\}$ (though only $k\le7$ is reliably fittable, above).
